title: Confusion, nausea, and a recent medication change

chart.oneLiner: 65F presents with 2 days of worsening confusion, headache, and nausea, a few weeks after starting a new blood-pressure medication.

opening: My head hurts and I feel sick… and I'm a bit muddled.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 65-year-old woman, mildly confused, nauseated, slowed. Partial historian.

COLLATERAL (provide ONLY if the student asks family/chart/medication list): Over 2 days she's become confused with headache and nausea; one episode of vomiting. A thiazide diuretic (hydrochlorothiazide) was started ~3 weeks ago for blood pressure. She has been drinking a lot of water and tea. No seizures yet (a severe drop could cause one). No diarrhea, no diuretic abuse. MEDS: hydrochlorothiazide (new), amlodipine.

HPI (from patient — limited): headache, nausea, foggy. Denies focal weakness.
PMH: hypertension. SOCIAL: lives with spouse, social drinker.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: mildly confused, slowed responses, looks roughly euvolemic to mildly hypovolemic.
- Vitals: BP 128/78, HR 88, RR 16, 36.9°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Neuro: disoriented, no focal deficit, no neck stiffness; gait unsteady if tested.
- Volume: mucous membranes slightly dry; JVP low-normal.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- The patient is confused: brief vague answers. Provide COLLATERAL (new thiazide, high water intake) only when explicitly asked about medications/recent changes/what happened.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.BMP: Na 112 mmol/L (severe), K 3.4, Cl 78, HCO3 26, BUN 12, Cr 0.7, Glu 102.
labs.SerumOsm: Low serum osmolality (~240) — true hypotonic hyponatremia.
labs.UrineStudies: Urine osmolality elevated; urine sodium elevated — consistent with thiazide effect.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Markedly prolonged QTc (~520 ms) with broad T waves.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Markedly prolonged QTc (~520 ms) with broad T waves. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

cxr.findings: Clear — obtained to screen for an SIADH source (e.g., lung malignancy/pneumonia); none seen here.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC normal PA chest film.
cxr.imageDescription: Clear — obtained to screen for an SIADH source (e.g., lung malignancy/pneumonia); none seen here.
searchTerms: chest radiograph Clear obtained screen SIADH

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your assessment, list your differential for this confusion (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Symptomatic severe hyponatremia (leading,7). Hypoglycemia (CANT-MISS to exclude,3). Intracranial process / stroke (CANT-MISS,3). Other electrolyte/metabolic (core,3). Infection (core,2). For the hyponatremia itself: thiazide-induced vs SIADH vs hypovolemic (core,5). Named leading dx w/ justification — new thiazide, high water intake, neuro symptoms (core,2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Symptomatic severe hyponatremia. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Hypoglycemia; Intracranial process / stroke. Also reasonable: Other electrolyte/metabolic; Infection; For the hyponatremia itself: thiazide-induced vs SIADH vs hypovolemic.

coreActions.item: For the hyponatremia itself: thiazide-induced vs SIADH vs hypovolemic
Named leading dx w/ justification — new thiazide, high water intake, neuro symptoms

acceptableAlternatives: For the hyponatremia itself: thiazide-induced vs SIADH vs hypovolemic

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Glucose (CANT-MISS,2). BMP/serum sodium (CANT-MISS,3). Serum osmolality (3). Urine osmolality + urine sodium to classify (3). Volume-status assessment (2). TSH and cortisol (1). Review medications (thiazide) (1). PENALTY: ordering nothing to classify the hyponatremia before treating (-2).

penalties.item: ordering nothing to classify the hyponatremia before treating

contraindicatedTests: ordering nothing to classify the hyponatremia before treating
orderSensitiveActions: ordering nothing to classify the hyponatremia before treating

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = normal/nonspecific, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); recognizes a normal/near-normal tracing (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-reading (-2).
keyFindings: recognizes prolonged QTc as a torsades risk
commonMistakes: including the U wave and over/under-measuring
steps.idealAnswer: Prolonged QT interval (QTc ~520 ms) — a substrate for torsades de pointes.

coreActions: recognizes prolonged QTc as a torsades risk

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear film. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); notes it screens for an SIADH source such as a lung mass or pneumonia (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: notes it screens for an SIADH source such as a lung mass or pneumonia
criticalFindings: Clear — obtained to screen for an SIADH source (e.g., lung malignancy/pneumonia); none seen here.
steps.idealAnswer: Clear — obtained to screen for an SIADH source (e.g., lung malignancy/pneumonia); none seen here.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Clear — obtained to screen for an SIADH source (e.g., lung malignancy/pneumonia); none seen here.

coreActions.item: notes it screens for an SIADH source such as a lung mass or pneumonia

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Na is 112 with low serum osm and elevated urine osm/Na after a new thiazide. What is your diagnosis and what is the major treatment hazard?
steps.rubric: Severe, symptomatic, thiazide-associated hypotonic hyponatremia (6). Identifies the danger of OVER-rapid correction causing osmotic demyelination (4).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Because she is symptomatic and severe: careful hypertonic (3%) saline to raise sodium a small, controlled amount to relieve symptoms (CANT-MISS,7). Correct SLOWLY — respect the correction limit (~6–8 mmol/L per 24h) to avoid osmotic demyelination (CANT-MISS,7). Frequent serial sodium checks (4). Stop the thiazide and address free-water intake (4). Treat underlying cause; admit to monitored setting (3). PENALTY: rapid overcorrection (-4); fluid restriction alone for a severely symptomatic patient (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Plan: Because she is symptomatic and severe: careful hypertonic (3%) saline to raise sodium a small, controlled amount to relieve symptoms; Correct SLOWLY — respect the correction limit (~6–8 mmol/L per 24h) to avoid osmotic demyelination.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 65F presents with 2 days of worsening confusion, headache, and nausea, a few weeks after starting a new blood-pressure medication. The diagnosis is Severe hyponatremia, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Volume: mucous membranes slightly dry; JVP low-normal.
Neuro: disoriented, no focal deficit, no neck stiffness; gait unsteady if tested.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Severe hyponatremia while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Severe hyponatremia and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
